title: EU Commission publishes final GPAI Code of Practice
summary: On May 4, 2026, the European Commission officially published the final version of the General-Purpose AI Code of Practice under the EU AI Act. Effective from August 2, 2026, the Code requires GPAI providers to disclose training data sources, conduct systemic risk assessments, and report serious incidents within 15 days. Signatories include OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and Mistral.

title: Why the EU's GPAI Code of Practice Won't Solve the Frontier Risk Problem
summary: The EU's final GPAI Code of Practice, while celebrated as the world's first binding industry code for frontier AI, suffers from three structural flaws: first, the systemic risk threshold of 10^25 FLOPs is already obsolete; second, the third-party audit clause lacks teeth; third, signatories retain unilateral exit rights. We argue the Code is more a political signal than a regulatory instrument.

title: The GPAI Code and the Myth of European AI Leadership
summary: Behind the celebratory press releases lies a sobering reality: the EU has neither the compute, the talent, nor the capital to lead AI governance through regulatory instruments alone. The new GPAI Code is a well-crafted document — and a politically convenient one — but it cannot substitute for industrial policy. Brussels risks regulating an industry it does not have.

title: Italy's Garante Fines OpenAI €15 Million for ChatGPT Data Protection Violations
summary: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) imposed a €15 million fine on OpenAI on May 1, 2026, for violations of GDPR Articles 5, 6, and 13 in connection with ChatGPT's processing of training data. The investigation, opened in March 2023, concluded that OpenAI failed to establish a valid legal basis for scraping personal data from public sources. OpenAI has 60 days to appeal.

title: FTC Finalizes COPPA Rule Update with New Restrictions on AI-Generated Content for Minors
summary: The Federal Trade Commission announced on April 28, 2026 the finalization of its COPPA Rule update. The new rule prohibits operators from using personal information of children under 13 to train generative AI models without verifiable parental consent, and bans behavioral advertising targeting minors. Compliance deadline: October 1, 2026.

title: ICO Publishes Final Age Assurance Guidance for Online Services
summary: The UK Information Commissioner's Office released its final Age Assurance Guidance on May 2, 2026, providing detailed expectations for online services likely to be accessed by children. The guidance establishes a 'risk-proportionate' standard, recommends specific age estimation methods, and requires DPIAs for any age assurance system processing biometric data.

title: NIST Releases AI RMF Generative AI Profile 2.0
summary: The National Institute of Standards and Technology released version 2.0 of its Generative AI Profile under the AI Risk Management Framework on April 30, 2026. The update introduces new guidance on synthetic content provenance, agentic AI risk taxonomies, and red-teaming protocols for foundation models above 10^25 FLOPs.

title: California Senate Bill 1047 Signed: AI Companion Apps Must Implement Suicide Prevention Protocols by January 2027
summary: California Governor signed SB 1047 into law on May 3, 2026, establishing the nation's first state-level requirements for AI companion applications. The law mandates suicide-prevention prompts when minors discuss self-harm, requires age verification for accounts, and imposes a $10,000-per-incident civil penalty for violations. Effective January 1, 2027.

title: The Quiet Rise of Agent Liability Regimes: A Comparative Analysis
summary: While headlines focus on the EU AI Act and US executive orders, a quieter regulatory front is opening: dedicated liability regimes for autonomous AI agents. Singapore's MDA, the UK's Law Commission, and Japan's METI have all issued consultation papers in the past 60 days. We compare three emerging models: principal-agent attribution, manufacturer strict liability, and the 'electronic person' approach.

title: Agentic AI and the Coming Liability Mess: Why 2026 Is the Year of the Lawsuit
summary: Anthropic's Claude can now operate a computer. OpenAI's Operator can buy you tickets. Google's Project Mariner browses the web on your behalf. None of the existing legal frameworks — agency law, product liability, consumer protection — were written with autonomous AI in mind. I predict 2026 will see at least three landmark lawsuits that will force courts to choose a doctrine. Here's my bet on which.

title: Texas SB 2420 Comes Into Effect: What Companies Need to Know About AI Disclosure Obligations
summary: Texas Senate Bill 2420, signed June 22, 2025, takes effect today, May 6, 2026. The law requires any business operating in Texas to disclose to consumers when AI is used to make 'consequential decisions' affecting employment, housing, healthcare, or financial services. The Texas Attorney General has been given enforcement authority with civil penalties up to $7,500 per violation.

title: Ofcom Launches Second Wave of Illegal Harms Code Enforcement: Five Platforms Under Investigation
summary: The UK communications regulator Ofcom announced on May 5, 2026, the opening of formal investigations against five major user-to-user services for failures under the Online Safety Act's Illegal Harms Codes of Practice. Affected platforms include two adult content sites, two messaging apps, and one image-sharing service. Maximum penalties under the Act reach 10% of global annual turnover.

title: Why AI Watermarking Is Failing — And What Should Replace It
summary: Three years after the major labs committed to robust watermarking under the White House voluntary commitments, independent audits show 80%+ removal rates with simple post-processing. The C2PA standard fares slightly better but suffers from low adoption. We argue the entire 'detect AI content' paradigm is misconceived, and propose a shift to provenance-by-design at the hardware layer.

title: US Restricts Nvidia H200 GPU Exports to China in Latest Chip Sanction Round
summary: The US Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security announced new export controls on May 4, 2026, restricting Nvidia H200 GPU shipments to entities in China. The measure expands the existing entity list and tightens the de minimis threshold for foreign-produced items. Industry groups warn of $20+ billion revenue impact for US chipmakers.

title: ASML Warns of 30% China Revenue Drop as Dutch Government Tightens Export Controls
summary: Dutch lithography equipment maker ASML disclosed in its Q1 earnings call that tightened export controls coordinated with the US BIS will reduce China-bound shipments of advanced lithography systems by 30% in Q2 2026. The announcement comes as the Dutch government adds three additional ASML product lines to its national export-control list.
